planting.note: An old New England maincrop celebrated for baking quality.

directions: Set callused seed potatoes about five inches deep and mound soil over the stems as they climb. Keep the plants evenly watered while the crop develops. Dig the floury white tubers in fall after the vines have died down for the best flavor.

planting.note: A late maincrop with outstanding blight resistance.

directions: Plant seed tubers five inches deep and give this tall, vigorous variety wide spacing. Earth up as needed, though its dense canopy smothers most weeds on its own. Harvest the red-skinned, floury tubers late; they resist blight in the ground and store for a long time.

planting.note: A yellow-fleshed Dutch maincrop popular for frites.

directions: Plant seed pieces five inches deep in fertile soil and hill the rows through the season. Water steadily to swell the oval, yellow-fleshed tubers. Lift after the tops die back for a heavy crop that fries and mashes well.

planting.note: A waxy second-early to maincrop good for salads.

directions: Set seed tubers four to five inches deep and earth up as the plants grow. Keep the soil moist so the long, yellow, waxy tubers stay firm and smooth. Harvest from late summer for boiling and salads once the foliage begins to fade.

planting.note: A rosy-skinned fingerling with yellow, waxy flesh.

directions: Set seed tubers four inches deep in loose soil and hill the rows as the plants develop. Keep watering steady during tuber set for smooth, curved fingerlings. Harvest the pink-skinned, yellow-fleshed tubers once the vines have died back for roasting and salads.

planting.note: A French gourmet fingerling with a nutty, chestnut flavor.

directions: Plant seed pieces four inches deep and spaced about ten inches apart, then hill as the plants grow. Water evenly so the knobby, curved tubers develop smoothly. Dig the small yellow fingerlings after the foliage dies for a prized, buttery salad potato.

planting.note: A long, crescent-shaped fingerling that yields well.

directions: Plant seed tubers four inches deep in fertile, loose soil and earth up the rows as vines climb. Keep the moisture steady to produce the long, curved tubers this variety is known for. Harvest the tan-skinned, yellow-fleshed fingerlings after the tops die back.

planting.note: A deep-purple heirloom fingerling with a long season.

directions: Plant seed pieces four inches deep and hill the rows through the season for this slow-maturing variety. Water evenly to encourage the slender purple tubers to size up. Harvest late once the foliage collapses; the flesh stays deep violet when cooked.

planting.note: A red-skinned, red-fleshed fingerling for color and salads.

directions: Set seed tubers four inches deep and space them close in the row, then hill as growth advances. Keep water steady so the small red fingerlings develop evenly. Harvest after the vines die back for waxy tubers with rosy skin and pink-tinged flesh.

planting.note: A blue-skinned, blue-fleshed variety high in antioxidants.

directions: Plant seed pieces about four inches deep and hill soil over the stems as the plants grow. Keep the bed evenly moist so the tubers size up well. Harvest the deep blue-purple potatoes after the tops yellow; the vivid color survives light steaming and roasting.

planting.note: A red-skinned variety with pink-red flesh.

directions: Set seed tubers four inches deep and mound soil around the plants as they grow. Water consistently during tuber formation for smooth, rosy potatoes. Dig once the foliage dies back; the pink flesh holds much of its color when cooked gently.

planting.note: A deep-purple Alaskan fingerling-type with rich color.

directions: Plant seed tubers four inches deep and hill the rows as the plants grow. Water evenly through the season so the elongated purple tubers develop fully. Harvest after the foliage dies back; the near-black flesh keeps its deep color when cooked.

planting.note: A purple-and-pink skinned variety with white flesh.

directions: Plant seed tubers four to five inches deep and earth up the rows as growth advances. Water consistently to swell the large, round tubers. Harvest after the vines die back; the striking purple skin hides creamy white flesh good for mashing.

planting.note: A red-skinned variety with rose-colored flesh.

directions: Set seed pieces four inches deep in loose soil and hill the rows as the plants grow. Keep water even so the tubers form smoothly. Dig the pink-fleshed potatoes after the tops die down; the rosy color holds up well when roasted.

planting.note: Also sold as Cranberry Red, with red skin and pink flesh.

directions: Plant seed tubers four inches deep and mound soil around the stems as the plants develop. Water steadily during tuber set for smooth, rosy potatoes. Harvest after the foliage dies back; the pink-marbled flesh adds color to boiled and roasted dishes.

planting.note: A nutty salad fingerling bred from Pink Fir Apple.

directions: Plant seed tubers four inches deep in loose soil and hill the rows as the plants grow. Keep watering even so the knobby, elongated tubers develop smoothly. Harvest the pink-tan fingerlings after the tops die back for a firm, nutty salad potato.

planting.note: A late heritage fingerling with knobby pink tubers.

directions: Plant seed tubers four inches deep and give this late variety a long, steady growing season. Hill the rows and water evenly so the knobby, pink-skinned tubers fill out. Harvest once the tops die back; the waxy yellow flesh is prized warm in salads.

planting.note: A popular waxy second early for salads and boiling.

directions: Plant chitted seed tubers four inches deep and earth up as the foliage grows. Keep the soil moist to swell the long, oval waxy tubers. Harvest from mid summer for firm, yellow-fleshed salad potatoes that hold their shape when boiled.

planting.note: A waxy second early that resists scab and blight well.

directions: Set seed tubers four inches deep and hill the rows as the plants develop. Water steadily so the small, firm tubers stay smooth and blemish free. Lift the creamy waxy potatoes from mid summer as tender new potatoes or a bit later for salads.

planting.note: A deeper-red-skinned selection of the early Norland.

directions: Plant seed pieces four inches deep early in the season and hill the rows as sprouts emerge. Water evenly so the smooth, dark-red tubers develop well. Harvest young as new potatoes about seven weeks in, or leave longer for a fuller early crop.

planting.note: A rosy fingerling also known as Rose Finn types, waxy and mild.

directions: Plant seed tubers four inches deep in loose soil and hill the rows through the season. Keep watering steady so the curved, rose-skinned fingerlings develop smoothly. Harvest after the vines die back for firm, yellow-fleshed tubers good for roasting.

planting.note: A red-skinned mid-season variety with good scab resistance.

directions: Set seed pieces four to five inches deep and earth up the rows as growth advances. Water steadily so the smooth red tubers develop cleanly. Harvest after the foliage begins to die back for a versatile boiling and mashing potato that stores well.

planting.note: Plant slips a few weeks after the last frost into warmed soil.

directions: Set slips into loose ridges spaced roughly a foot apart, burying the stem to the top leaves. This carrot-colored heirloom adapts to many soils and stores well after curing. Dig the tapered roots before frost once the vines have run.

planting.note: Transplant once soil is warm and settled after the last frost.

directions: Plant slips deeply in warm ridged soil, watering steadily until they take hold. This copper-skinned, moist orange variety is a longtime favorite for baking and stores dependably. Harvest before frost and cure the roots warm to sweeten them.

planting.note: Set slips out after the last frost when the ground has warmed.

directions: Bury slips to the top leaves in loose, free-draining ridges. This dark red-skinned, deep-orange variety stays notably moist and sweet when baked. Lift the roots before frost and cure them for best flavor and storage.

planting.note: Plant slips into warm soil a few weeks past the last frost.

directions: Set slips deeply into ridged beds spaced about a foot apart. This old Southern heirloom has yellow skin and soft, juicy golden flesh that turns very sweet with curing. Dig the roots before frost and store them in a warm, dry place.

planting.note: Transplant slips into warm soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Set slips deeply into loose ridged beds spaced a foot apart. This variety has smooth cream-tan skin and white flesh with a drier, mild texture. Dig the roots before frost and cure them warm before storing.

planting.note: Set out slips after the soil has warmed past the last frost.

directions: Plant slips to the top leaves in warm, well-drained ridges. A white-fleshed sport of Beauregard, it offers a milder, less sugary, creamy flavor. Harvest before frost and cure the roots for good keeping quality.

planting.note: Needs a long, hot season; plant slips well after the last frost.

directions: Plant slips into warm ridged soil and be patient, as this variety needs a long hot season. The tan skin hides vivid purple flesh that keeps its color and turns sweet when cooked. Harvest before frost and cure warm for storage.

planting.note: A long-season purple type; plant slips into fully warmed soil.

directions: Set slips deeply into loose ridged beds in a spot with a long, warm growing season. Both skin and flesh are deep purple, and the roots bake up dense and only mildly sweet. Dig before frost and cure the roots to improve texture and flavor.

planting.note: A space-saving bush type; plant slips after frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant slips into warm ridges; the compact vines stay short and are ideal where space is tight. This heirloom produces copper-skinned roots with sweet orange flesh good for baking. Harvest before frost and cure the roots warm before storing.

planting.note: Set out slips once soil has warmed after the last frost.

directions: Plant slips deeply into loose ridged beds spaced about a foot apart. This variety produces smooth, uniform copper roots with deep orange flesh well suited to baking. Dig the roots before frost and cure them to deepen sweetness.

planting.note: Plant slips into warmed soil a few weeks after the last frost.

directions: Set slips deeply into ridged beds and keep them watered while establishing. This Louisiana variety is prized for unusually high sugar content and orange flesh. Harvest before frost and cure the roots to develop their signature sweetness.

planting.note: Transplant slips once the soil is dependably warm.

directions: Plant slips into loose warm ridges spaced a foot apart. This variety shows off very dark red skin over sweet orange flesh. Harvest before frost and cure the roots warm and humid to firm them for storage.

planting.note: Set out slips after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant slips deeply into ridged beds and keep the soil evenly moist early on. This variety was bred for pest resistance and produces purplish-red skinned roots with orange flesh. Dig before frost and cure the roots to sweeten them.

planting.note: Plant slips into warm soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Set slips deeply into loose ridged soil spaced about a foot apart. This vining heirloom is a classic Southern baking type with copper skin and orange flesh. Harvest before frost and cure the roots warm for best flavor.

planting.note: Set out slips after the soil warms following the last frost.

directions: Plant slips into warm ridged beds and water steadily while they root in. This Southern heirloom yields sweet, moist orange-fleshed roots long favored for pies and baking. Dig before frost and cure the roots warm to concentrate their sugars.

planting.note: Plant slips into warmed soil a few weeks after the last frost.

directions: Set slips deeply into loose ridged beds spaced about a foot apart. This variety has rich burgundy-red skin over sweet, deep-orange flesh. Harvest before frost and cure the roots to firm the skins and boost sweetness.

planting.note: Transplant slips once the soil is dependably warm after frost.

directions: Plant slips deeply into warm, well-drained ridges spaced a foot apart. This newer release produces smooth, uniform orange-fleshed roots with good yield and storage. Dig before frost and cure the roots warm and humid before storing.

planting.note: A long-season Hawaiian type; plant slips into fully warmed soil.

directions: Set slips into loose warm ridges where they will get a long, hot season. This Hawaiian variety carries deep purple flesh that holds its color and stays mildly sweet when cooked. Harvest before frost and cure the roots warm for storage.

planting.note: Sow directly once the soil can be worked in spring, as roots need a long season.

directions: Sow fresh seed about a half inch deep into deeply dug, stone-free soil and keep the surface moist during the slow three-to-four-week germination. Thin seedlings to a hand's width apart so the long tapered roots can size up. Lift after the first hard frosts, which sweeten the flesh, or leave roots in the ground and dig through winter.

planting.note: Direct sow in early to mid spring for autumn and winter roots.

directions: Plant seed shallowly in loose, well-worked ground and firm it gently to hold moisture. Keep beds weeded while the slow seedlings establish, then thin to give each smooth uniform root room to swell. Harvest from mid autumn onward, digging as needed once cold weather improves the sweetness.

planting.note: Sow this vigorous F1 hybrid in spring as soon as the ground is workable.

directions: Drill seed a half inch deep into deep, fertile soil that has not been freshly manured, which can cause forking. This hybrid germinates more evenly than open-pollinated types, so thin promptly to a few inches apart. Dig the smooth wedge-shaped roots after frost has sweetened them, from autumn through winter.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring for long, tapered white roots by fall.

directions: Sow shallowly into loose ground dug deeply enough to accommodate long roots, and keep evenly moist through the drawn-out germination. Thin seedlings so the smooth white roots have space to lengthen without crowding. Begin lifting in autumn, and let roots overwinter in mild soil for a sweeter winter crop.

planting.note: Sow in spring; this variety shows good canker resistance in wet soils.

directions: Set seed a half inch deep in deeply cultivated soil and firm lightly to retain moisture during germination. The medium-length roots have smooth shoulders that resist canker, so this type suits heavier or damper ground. Lift from autumn onward, digging through winter as the flavor deepens with cold.

planting.note: A long-rooted heirloom; sow in spring in deep, loose soil.

directions: Because the roots run long and slender, dig the bed deeply and remove stones before sowing seed a half inch down. Keep the surface damp during germination and thin the slow seedlings to a few inches apart. Harvest the sweet, near-coreless roots in autumn and winter after frost has passed over the bed.

planting.note: Sow this canker-resistant F1 hybrid in spring once soil warms slightly.

directions: Sow the hybrid seed a half inch deep in deep, stone-free soil for straight, well-shaped roots. This selection resists canker well, making it reliable on damper ground where older types can rot. Thin early, then dig the smooth roots from autumn through winter as they sweeten.

planting.note: Sow this smooth-skinned F1 hybrid in spring for uniform winter roots.

directions: Plant seed shallowly in deeply worked ground and keep it moist through the extended germination window. This hybrid produces long, clean-shouldered white roots with reliable uniformity across the row. Lift from mid autumn onward, leaving some roots to overwinter for the sweetest eating.

planting.note: An heirloom noted for flavor; direct sow in spring.

directions: Sow seed a half inch deep into deep, fertile soil and keep the bed evenly moist until seedlings appear. Thin to a few inches apart so the flavorful roots can develop full size. Prized for its sweet, nutty taste, this variety is best dug after autumn frosts and through winter.

planting.note: A compact, canker-resistant variety; sow in spring, close spacing tolerated.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and grow the small, cone-shaped roots at closer spacing than long types will allow. Its strong canker resistance makes it a dependable choice on heavier or wetter soils. Harvest the sweet, tender roots from autumn onward once cold weather has set in.

planting.note: Sow directly in spring; the long taproots need a full season to mature.

directions: Sow fresh seed about a half inch deep in deep, loose soil free of fresh manure to prevent the long roots from forking. Thin seedlings to a couple of inches apart and keep the bed weeded through the growing season. Lift the slender, oyster-flavored roots after autumn frost, or leave them in the ground and dig through winter as needed.

planting.note: Sow in spring; selected for uniform, smooth long roots.

directions: Drill seed shallowly into deep, stone-free soil to encourage the long cylindrical roots to run straight. Keep the bed moist and weed-free during the season to avoid tough, fibrous flesh. Lift the dark-skinned roots in autumn, handling them gently since they bleed sap and break easily.

planting.note: A large-rooted heirloom selection; sow directly in spring.

directions: Sow seed a half inch deep in loose, deeply worked ground so the thick black roots can reach full length. Thin plants and keep the soil consistently damp for tender flesh. Dig the long roots in autumn, or overwinter them in place and harvest as the ground allows.

planting.note: An heirloom with short, thick roots; sow directly in early spring.

directions: Sow shallowly into well-worked soil and keep the surface moist through the slow germination period. This selection makes shorter, stockier roots that suit heavier ground where long types struggle. Dig the aromatic roots from autumn into winter, using them in soups and stews much like a small parsnip.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; the deep roots need loose soil worked two feet down.

directions: Sow seed a half inch deep in deeply loosened, sandy soil, since the slender roots of this gobo can drive two to three feet down. Thin seedlings and water steadily to keep the roots tender rather than fibrous. Dig the long roots in autumn of the first year, loosening a deep trench alongside to lift them without snapping.

planting.note: An earlier-maturing gobo; direct sow after frost in deep, loose soil.

directions: Sow directly into deeply dug soil once frost has passed, spacing seed for straight, unforked roots. This selection matures somewhat earlier than standard gobo, so roots can be lifted from late summer. Harvest by digging a deep trench beside the row and easing the long roots free before they turn woody.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a biennial grown for its first-year taproot.

directions: Sow seed a half inch deep in deeply worked, well-drained soil, as the taproot grows long and needs room to descend. Keep plants watered through the season and harvest the roots in autumn of the first year before the plant bolts and sends up its burred flower stalk. Dig alongside a loosened trench to lift the brittle roots intact.

planting.note: Plant tubers in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked.

directions: Plant tubers about 4 inches deep in early spring; the tall sunflower relatives are vigorous and best confined to a dedicated bed. 'Fuseau' produces smoother, less knobbly tubers that are easier to clean than wild types. Harvest after frost blackens the stems, digging tubers as needed since they keep best in the ground over winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers in early spring in a permanent spot.

directions: Bury tubers about 4 inches deep in spring and expect a dense, towering stand that can spread aggressively from any tuber left behind. Late-summer yellow flowers precede the crop of knobby underground tubers. Dig after the first hard frost and continue harvesting through winter, as the tubers store well in cold ground.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring in deep, stone-free soil.

directions: Sow seed about half an inch deep in deeply dug soil free of stones so the long taproots grow straight. Keep the bed weeded and evenly watered through the season for tender, oyster-flavored roots. Harvest after the first frosts, which sweeten the roots; they can be left in the ground and dug through winter.

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings after frost in a long-season site.

directions: Root stem cuttings and set them out after frost in deep, fertile soil for this rare highland Andean crop. The plant needs a long season to develop both edible thickened crowns and fleshy storage roots. Harvest the roots in autumn and cure them in the sun for several days to sweeten their starch.

planting.note: Sow seed in warm soil after frost; a bushier relative of jicama.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in warm soil; unlike jicama this Andean yam bean is compact and largely self-supporting. Remove flower buds to push growth into the crisp, sweet taproot. Dig the roots before frost and eat only the tuber, since the seeds and pods contain toxic compounds.

planting.note: A long-day type best for northern gardens; start indoors and transplant in early spring.

directions: Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before setting out and space the transplants generously to give these large globes room. Feed with a nitrogen source through early growth and keep the soil consistently moist until the bulbs size up. Lift when the necks soften and the tops flop, then cure well since flavor is mild and storage only fair.

planting.note: Traditionally fall-sown in mild areas and overwintered, or spring-started in colder zones.

directions: For the largest bulbs, sow in late summer where winters are mild and overwinter the young plants under mulch, or start indoors in late winter in colder regions. Water freely since this juicy, low-sulfur onion resents dry spells. Harvest in early summer once the tops tip over; the sweet, mild flesh bruises easily and keeps only a few weeks.

planting.note: A short-day onion for the South; set transplants in autumn to bulb the following spring.

directions: In southern gardens, set out transplants in fall so the plants grow leaves through the cool months before bulbing under the short days of spring. Keep the beds fed and weeded and water steadily until the bulbs are full. Harvest in spring when the tops fall; the flesh is very sweet and mild but stores poorly, so use it fresh.

planting.note: The classic Vidalia-type onion; a short-day variety for the Deep South.

directions: Set transplants into fertile, well-drained soil in fall in the South and let them establish over winter. Provide steady moisture and nitrogen through the growing months to build the broad, flattened bulb. Harvest in late spring when the necks weaken; the flesh is famously mild and sweet but short-lived in storage.

planting.note: A hard, high-solids long-day hybrid prized for long keeping in northern gardens.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant into rich soil as early as the ground can be worked. Keep growth steady with even water and moderate feeding, then let the soil dry as the tops mature. Harvest once the necks are thin and the tops down, and cure thoroughly; these dense yellow bulbs keep well into winter.

planting.note: A long-day yellow storage hybrid, one of the best keepers for the North.

directions: Sow indoors eight to ten weeks ahead and set out sturdy seedlings in early spring. Grow on with regular water and keep the bed clean until the medium-sized bulbs finish sizing. Harvest when the tops fall and cure the firm, thin-necked bulbs well; they routinely store many months without sprouting.

planting.note: A long-day yellow hybrid with good size and dependable storage.

directions: Begin seed indoors in late winter and transplant into loose, fertile ground once workable. Water evenly and feed early, tapering off as the tops begin to yield. Lift when the necks soften, then cure the round, straw-colored bulbs in a dry place for months of storage.

planting.note: A heritage long-day onion grown for very large show-quality bulbs.

directions: Sow early indoors, even in midwinter for exhibition, and grow the seedlings on before spacing them widely in rich, deeply worked soil. Feed and water generously to push size, keeping the bulbs uncrowded. Harvest in late summer when the tops bend; the huge globes are mild but keep only a short time.

planting.note: A flat yellow keeper most often grown from sets in early spring.

directions: Press sets root-down about an inch deep as soon as the soil can be worked, or start seed indoors for transplants. Keep the row weeded and watered while the flat, coppery bulbs develop. Harvest when the tops fall and cure well; this old variety is a reliable long keeper.

planting.note: A late Italian long-day storage onion with dense, top-shaped bulbs.

directions: Because it matures late, start seed indoors early and transplant as soon as the ground allows. Give it a full season of steady moisture and feeding to fill the golden, teardrop bulbs. Harvest late when the tops collapse, then cure the firm onions for excellent winter storage.

planting.note: A short-day red for southern gardens, mild and quick to size.

directions: Set transplants in fall in the South, or in earliest spring farther north, into fertile, well-drained soil. Keep the plants moist and weed-free as the flattened red globes form. Harvest when the tops soften and fall; the sweet, mild flesh is best fresh since it keeps only briefly.

planting.note: A heirloom long-day red with firm flesh and good keeping.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant into rich ground as early as it can be worked. Water evenly through the season while the large, flat-topped red bulbs develop. Harvest when the tops fall over and cure well; this old variety stores better than most reds and holds its pungent flavor.

planting.note: A long-day red with deep color throughout the rings.

directions: Sow indoors and set out transplants in early spring, or press sets shallowly once the soil warms. Keep the bed watered and weeded while the round, dark red bulbs fill. Harvest when the tops flop and cure the onions well; they hold their strong color and store for several months.

planting.note: A long-day red hybrid that keeps unusually well for a red onion.

directions: Begin seed indoors in late winter and transplant into fertile soil as soon as the ground is workable. Grow with steady moisture and modest feeding, letting the beds dry as the tops mature. Harvest when the necks weaken; the hard, uniform red bulbs cure well and hold their color deep into storage.

planting.note: A pungent short-day red that stores unusually well for the South.

directions: Set transplants in fall in southern gardens into well-drained, fertile soil and grow them through the cool season. Keep them watered and weeded as the small, thick, flattened bulbs harden off. Harvest in spring when the tops fall; the sharp red flesh is dense and keeps far longer than most southern onions.

planting.note: An heirloom long-day red with true globe shape and firm flesh.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and set transplants out in early spring into rich soil. Keep the plants evenly watered while the round, deep-red globes size up. Harvest when the tops bend and yellow, then cure the firm bulbs well for good winter keeping.

planting.note: An Italian long-day red with barrel-shaped, well-keeping bulbs.

directions: Sow indoors early and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil once the ground is workable. Water steadily as the upright, drum-shaped red bulbs form. Harvest when the tops fall; this variety cures to a firm, glossy skin and stores better than many reds.

planting.note: An Italian torpedo-shaped sweet red, mild and quick to mature.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant into loose, fertile soil in spring. Keep moisture even as the elongated, torpedo-shaped red bulbs develop. Harvest young for scallion-like use or let the bulbs mature; the sweet, mild flesh is best enjoyed fresh since it stores only briefly.

planting.note: The classic golden Italian cipollini, a small flat onion for roasting.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant closely, since these little onions are meant to stay small and flat. Grow with even moisture and light feeding as the disc-shaped bulbs form. Harvest when the tops fall; the sweet, high-sugar flesh caramelizes beautifully and the cured bulbs keep for a few months.

planting.note: A small flat red cipollini-type prized for roasting whole.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant closely so the bulbs stay small and flattened. Keep the bed evenly moist and weeded as the little red discs develop. Harvest when the tops soften; the sweet flesh roasts and pickles well and cures for modest storage.

planting.note: A quick small purple onion good as a baby bulb or pickler.

directions: Sow seed directly a half inch deep as soon as the soil can be worked and thin lightly for small bulbs. Keep the row moist for tender, quick growth. Pull young for purple-skinned pearl onions or let them size to a small bulb; the color fades to pale pink when cooked, and they keep only briefly.

planting.note: A large mild white long-day onion for northern gardens.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant into rich soil in early spring. Give steady water and feeding as the big, round white globes form. Harvest when the tops fall; the mild sweet flesh is excellent fresh but keeps only a short time, so use it soon after curing.

planting.note: A heirloom long-day white with firm globe bulbs and good storage.

directions: Sow indoors early and set out transplants in spring into fertile, well-drained soil. Keep the plants watered while the round white bulbs develop firm flesh. Harvest when the tops bend and cure the onions well; this old variety keeps better than most whites.

planting.note: A short-day flat white Bermuda-type for the South, good for pickling.

directions: Set transplants in fall in southern gardens into loose, fertile soil and grow through the cool season. Keep them moist and weeded as the flat, waxy white bulbs form. Harvest young for pickling onions or let them mature; the mild flesh is best used fresh since it keeps only briefly.

planting.note: An intermediate-day sweet hybrid that bulbs across a wide latitude range.

directions: Because it is day-neutral, this onion succeeds in middle latitudes where long- and short-day types struggle; set transplants out in spring. Keep the soil evenly moist and fed as the large, mild bulbs form. Harvest when the tops fall; the sweet flesh is best fresh and stores only a couple of months.

planting.note: An intermediate-day red hybrid suited to the central states.

directions: Set transplants out in spring into fertile, well-drained soil; as a day-neutral type it bulbs reliably in mid-latitude gardens. Water steadily while the round red globes size up. Harvest when the tops soften and fall, then cure well; the firm bulbs store for a few months.

planting.note: A dependable yellow storage onion most often grown from sets.

directions: Push sets root-down about an inch deep in early spring so just the tip shows, or start from seed for transplants. Keep the row weeded and watered as the round yellow bulbs develop. Harvest when the tops fall and cure well; this reliable variety stores through much of the winter.

planting.note: A long-day exhibition onion capable of enormous, sweet bulbs.

directions: For record size, sow indoors in midwinter and grow the seedlings on before spacing them widely in deeply enriched soil. Water and feed generously through the season to push the bulbs, keeping them uncrowded. Harvest in late summer when the tops bend; the giant globes are notably sweet but store only a short time.

planting.note: A long-day yellow storage hybrid with large, uniform globes.

directions: Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before transplanting into fertile soil in early spring. Grow steadily with even water, letting the bed dry as the tops finish. Harvest when the necks soften and the tops fall, then cure the firm yellow globes for long, dependable storage.

planting.note: A large white long-day Spanish-type onion, mild and firm.

directions: Sow indoors in late winter and transplant into rich soil once the ground can be worked. Keep the plants evenly watered as the big white globes fill out. Harvest when the tops fall; the mild flesh slices well fresh and keeps moderately after a thorough cure.

planting.note: A reliable heritage long-day yellow onion with good keeping.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and set transplants out in early spring, or sow directly as soon as the soil warms. Grow on with even moisture and light feeding as the round straw-colored bulbs develop. Harvest when the tops fall, then cure the firm onions well for dependable winter storage.

planting.note: An early-maturing long-day yellow that still keeps well.

directions: Sow indoors in late winter and transplant into fertile soil early in spring for a quick northern crop. Keep the bed moist and weeded while the flattened yellow bulbs size up. Harvest when the tops fall over, then cure thoroughly; this early variety stores surprisingly well for its short season.

planting.note: A small flat golden cipollini-type selected for roasting whole.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant closely so the bulbs stay small and disc-shaped. Grow with even moisture and light feeding as the flat golden onions form. Harvest when the tops fall; the high sugar content makes them caramelize richly, and cured bulbs keep for a few months.

planting.note: Plant cloves in fall; in mild-winter regions late-fall planting still works well.

directions: Separate cloves just before planting and set them two inches deep in loose, well-drained soil. This is a dependable commercial softneck that matures earlier than most; feed with nitrogen in early spring and taper off water as bulbs size up. Harvest once several lower leaves brown and the tops begin to soften and fall.

planting.note: Set cloves out in autumn for a mid-to-late summer crop.

directions: Plant individual cloves point-up about two inches below the surface in fertile ground. Its tightly wrapped bulbs cure and braid nicely and hold in storage for many months. Dig this later-maturing softneck when roughly half the foliage has yellowed, then cure in a shaded, airy spot.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; this heirloom tolerates warmer winters better than most softnecks.

directions: Push cloves two inches deep into rich, loose soil and cover with a light mulch. A long-cultivated Italian-American heirloom, it grows large bulbs with a robust, lingering pungency and adapts to hot summers. Harvest when the outer leaves dry and bend over, then braid the pliable stems for storage.

planting.note: Plant cloves in autumn for harvest the following summer.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep with the flat basal plate facing down. This Republic-of-Georgia heirloom softneck has a mild, sweet flavor prized for eating raw and adapts across many climates. Lift the bulbs when the tops begin to lean and the lower leaves have gone brown.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves; a productive softneck for a wide range of regions.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep in well-worked soil and keep the bed weed-free. This reliable artichoke type resists sunscald and yields uniform bulbs with a mild, well-rounded heat. Harvest as the lower leaves dry and several upper leaves remain green, then cure thoroughly.

planting.note: Set cloves out in fall for an early-to-midsummer harvest.

directions: Space cloves four to six inches apart and plant them two inches deep, tip pointing skyward. This Eastern European softneck produces plump, easy-to-peel cloves with a balanced flavor and stores through winter. Dig the bulbs when about half the leaves have browned and dried down.

planting.note: Plant cloves in autumn; this softneck adapts to both cold and mild winters.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep and mulch to hold moisture and steady soil temperature. This adaptable artichoke type has rose-tinted wrappers and a bright, medium heat, and it braids readily for hanging storage. Harvest when the tops start to fall and lower leaves have dried.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves; a cold-tolerant softneck also sold as New York White.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and cover with mulch for winter protection. This hardy softneck yields large white bulbs with a strong, spreading flavor and excellent keeping quality. Harvest once the lower leaves brown and the necks begin to soften.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves of this long-storing silverskin type.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep in loose soil and keep the bed evenly moist through spring. This Pacific Northwest heirloom silverskin has rose-streaked wrappers and offers some of the longest storage life of any garlic. Harvest as the tops begin to fall over, then cure and braid the strong necks.

planting.note: Set cloves out in autumn; a classic late-maturing silverskin for storage.

directions: Push cloves two inches into the soil with the pointed end up and mulch lightly. This silverskin has silvery-white bulbs blushed with rose and holds firm well into the following spring. Lift the bulbs when the necks weaken and the tops flop, then cure in a dry, airy place before braiding.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves of this productive white silverskin.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep in well-drained ground and keep weeds down through the season. This vigorous silverskin produces glossy white bulbs that braid beautifully and store for the better part of a year. Harvest when the tops soften and bend, and cure fully before trimming.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves of this large-bulbed heirloom softneck.

directions: Give this large type a little extra space and plant cloves two inches deep in rich soil. A Washington-state heirloom, it forms unusually big bulbs with a mild, agreeable flavor and stores dependably. Harvest once the lower leaves yellow and several upper leaves stay green, then cure well.

planting.note: Set cloves in autumn; a late silverskin favored for braiding.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep and firm the soil gently around them. This silverskin has long, flexible tops that make handsome braids and cloves with a soft, mild bite. Harvest when the foliage falls over and dries, then hang to cure in good airflow.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; this cold-loving rocambole needs a real winter chill.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep in autumn and mulch after the ground cools. As a hardneck it throws a curling flower scape in late spring; snip the scapes off to steer energy into the bulb and enjoy them cooked. This rocambole has a rich, full-bodied flavor and peels easily; harvest when the lower few leaves brown.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves; performs best where winters are genuinely cold.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep and cover the bed with straw once the soil chills. Remove the coiled scapes in early summer to boost bulb size. A classic rocambole heirloom, it is famous for a deep, true garlic flavor and skins that slip off easily; lift the bulbs when the lower leaves have dried.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves of this disease-resistant rocambole.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep in loose, fertile soil in fall and mulch for winter. This rocambole is noted for vigor and good tolerance of soilborne problems, with a strong, savory flavor. Cut scapes when they curl, then harvest bulbs as the bottom leaves yellow.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves; a hardy rocambole suited to cold climates.

directions: Plant cloves point-up two inches deep and protect with mulch over winter. This robust rocambole brought north by settlers produces large cloves with a hot, hearty flavor that mellows when cooked. Remove scapes as they appear, and dig bulbs when a few lower leaves have browned.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves of this cold-hardy Eastern European rocambole.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep in autumn and mulch heavily against deep frost. Originating in the Carpathian mountains, this rocambole is very winter-tough and yields fat, easy-peeling cloves with a bold flavor. Snap off scapes when they loop, then harvest as the lower leaves dry.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves; a large-bulbed rocambole for cold regions.

directions: Give this vigorous type a bit of extra spacing and plant cloves two inches deep. It forms large bulbs with big, peel-friendly cloves and the deep flavor typical of rocamboles. Cut the curling scapes for the kitchen, then harvest bulbs once the bottom leaves turn brown.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; an extremely hardy porcelain type for cold winters.

directions: Space these large cloves six to eight inches apart and plant two inches deep in fall. Porcelain garlics hold only a few big cloves per bulb wrapped in thick white skin and store very well. Remove scapes in early summer, and harvest when the lower leaves brown while upper foliage stays green.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves of this hardy porcelain, also called German Extra Hardy.

directions: Plant the large cloves two inches deep and mulch well for winter. This porcelain type is among the most cold-hardy garlics and delivers a strong, full flavor with excellent storage. Snip the scapes as they emerge, then lift bulbs when several lower leaves have dried.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; a hot, hardy porcelain from the country of Georgia.

directions: Set the big cloves two inches deep in rich soil and cover with mulch. This porcelain is prized for a fiery raw punch that softens with cooking, plus reliable storage. Cut scapes to size the bulbs, then harvest when the lower third of the leaves turns brown.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves of this mild, hardy porcelain type.

directions: Plant the large cloves two inches deep and firm the soil around them before mulching. Despite the porcelain type's usual heat, this one is notably mild and pleasant even eaten raw, with big cloves and strong storage. Remove scapes in early summer and harvest when the bottom leaves have yellowed.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; a pungent, very hardy porcelain.

directions: Set the large cloves two inches deep in fall and mulch after the soil cools. This porcelain forms fat cloves with an intense, hot flavor and heavy raw pungency that appeals to strong-garlic lovers. Cut scapes to help bulbs size up, then harvest as the lower leaves brown.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves of this cold-climate porcelain.

directions: Plant the large cloves two inches deep in well-drained soil and mulch for winter. This porcelain performs well in northern gardens, producing clean white bulbs with a few big cloves and long storage. Remove scapes when they curl, and harvest as the lower leaves dry down.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves of this vigorous, uniform porcelain.

directions: Set large cloves two inches deep and space them generously for good bulb size. This porcelain is valued for consistent, handsome bulbs with a strong, warm flavor and dependable keeping. Snap scapes off in early summer, then harvest when several lower leaves have browned.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves; a hardy porcelain with rosy clove skins.

directions: Plant the large cloves two inches deep in fall and cover the bed with mulch. This porcelain forms a few big cloves wrapped in rose-tinted skin, with a robust flavor and long shelf life. Remove scapes to direct energy downward, and harvest as the lower leaves yellow.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves of this classic purple stripe from the country of Georgia.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep in autumn and mulch once the ground chills. Purple stripe types show striking violet streaking on their wrappers; this one is a renowned roasting garlic that turns sweet and creamy when cooked. Cut scapes as they curl, then harvest when the lower leaves brown.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves; a purple stripe also known as Samarkand.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep and protect with mulch through winter. This purple stripe, collected in a Central Asian bazaar, has purple-marked cloves and a mild, spicy flavor with a pleasant zing. Remove scapes in early summer, and harvest as the lower leaves dry.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves of this hardy purple stripe.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep in loose soil and mulch for the winter. This purple stripe hails from a mountain village and is a hardy, productive grower with a clean, warm flavor. Snap scapes off as they loop, then harvest when the bottom leaves have browned.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves of this glazed purple stripe type.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep in fall and cover with mulch after the soil cools. Glazed purple stripes shine with a metallic sheen on their wrappers; this one has a mild, sweet flavor good for roasting. Cut scapes when they curl, and harvest as the lower leaves yellow.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves of this hardy glazed purple stripe.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep and mulch for winter protection. This glazed purple stripe is a strong grower with shimmering wrappers and a rich, well-balanced flavor. Remove scapes as they appear, then harvest when several lower leaves have browned.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves; a very hardy marbled purple stripe.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep in fall and mulch heavily against deep cold. Marbled purple stripes are among the hardiest and best-storing hardnecks; this one is fiery and strong when raw. Cut scapes to size the bulb, then harvest when the lower leaves brown.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves of this pungent marbled purple stripe.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep and mulch once the soil chills. This Russian marbled purple stripe is intensely hot and one of the longest-storing hardnecks. Remove scapes in early summer, and harvest when the lower third of leaves has dried.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves; an extremely cold-hardy marbled purple stripe.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep in fall and protect with a thick winter mulch. This marbled purple stripe shrugs off severe cold and produces plump cloves with a medium, well-rounded flavor. Cut scapes when they curl, then harvest as the lower leaves brown.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; an early-maturing asiatic type.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep in autumn and mulch for winter. Asiatic types mature early and carry weak, floppy scapes; leave a few standing since bulbs may finish sooner than other hardnecks. This one is notably hot and spicy raw; harvest promptly when the lower leaves brown, as asiatics can split if left too long.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves of this early asiatic type.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep and mulch once the ground cools. This asiatic garlic matures early with rosy clove skins and a bright, spicy bite that fades quickly. Watch the crop closely and harvest as soon as the lower leaves brown, since asiatics tend to mature fast and can burst their wrappers.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; turban types are the earliest garlics to mature.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep in autumn and give a light winter mulch. Turban garlics are the first to finish each season and do well even in milder-winter areas. This one has purple-blushed cloves and a mild flavor; harvest early, right when the lower leaves brown, so the wrappers stay intact.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves of this very early turban type.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep and mulch lightly for winter. This turban matures very early with colorful, easy-peeling cloves and a mild-to-medium flavor. Because turbans do not store long, harvest promptly when the lower leaves brown and use the bulbs within a few months.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; an early, colorful turban type.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep in fall and cover with a thin mulch. This turban ripens early and shows attractive red-streaked wrappers over mild, quick-to-peel cloves. Harvest as soon as the lower leaves brown, and plan to use the short-storing bulbs within a season.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; creole types thrive in mild-winter and southern gardens.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep in well-drained soil; creoles need less winter chill and suit warmer regions where hardnecks struggle. This group produces vivid rose-red bulbs with a rich, mildly sweet flavor and superb storage. Harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure thoroughly for long keeping.

planting.note: Autumn-plant cloves of this creole type favored in warm climates.

directions: Set cloves two inches deep in loose, well-drained soil in fall. This creole garlic tolerates warm winters and forms deep red-skinned cloves with a rich, complex flavor and long shelf life. Harvest when the lower leaves brown, and cure the bulbs well before storing.

planting.note: Fall-plant cloves; a creole type well suited to southern gardens.

directions: Plant cloves two inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil. This creole needs little winter chill and produces striking burgundy-tinted bulbs with a mild, sweet, and pleasantly complex flavor. Harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure to bring out the best storage.

planting.note: Fall-plant the large cloves; botanically a leek relative grown like garlic.

directions: Give each big clove plenty of room and plant three to four inches deep in rich soil. Though sold as garlic, this is really a leek relative, forming very large bulbs of a few huge cloves with a mild, faintly garlicky flavor. Remove the tall flower stalk to boost bulb size, and harvest when the lower leaves yellow and flop.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set hardened seedlings out several weeks before the last frost.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep indoors, then transplant seedlings into a four-inch furrow and let irrigation gradually fill it to blanch the stems. Draw soil up around the shanks every few weeks to lengthen the white portion. Harvest once stems reach an inch or more across, easing the roots up with a fork.

planting.note: An early summer leek best started indoors and set out in mid-spring for a fall crop.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant pencil-thick seedlings into deep dibbed holes, watering them in without backfilling. This fast, tall variety needs little hilling to produce long pale shanks. Pull leeks while young and tender since this type does not stand hard winters as well as blue-leaved kinds.

planting.note: A hardy heirloom that stands through winter; start early and transplant in spring.

directions: Sow indoors, transplant into rich soil, and hill the thick stems progressively through the season. This vigorous old Scottish leek forms broad shanks and holds well in the ground through cold weather. Dig as needed from autumn into winter wherever the ground does not freeze solid.

planting.note: A very cold-hardy overwintering leek for the latest harvests.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and set plants deeply to encourage long blanched shanks. Its blue-green foliage resists cold, letting stalks stand in the garden into winter. Harvest from late fall onward, lifting stems as the kitchen needs them.

planting.note: A French heirloom whose blue leaves turn violet in cold; overwinters reliably.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant seedlings into deep holes, hilling soil around the stems for length. The steel-blue foliage takes on a purple cast after frost and endures hard cold. Lift the thick, mild-flavored shanks through the winter months.

planting.note: A productive mid-season leek for summer and fall harvest.

directions: Start seed indoors and move sturdy seedlings to deep dibbed holes once they reach pencil thickness. Keep the bed weed-free and evenly moist for straight, upright shanks. Begin pulling when stems thicken in late summer and continue through autumn.

planting.note: Press individual sets into worked ground as early as it can be dug in spring.

directions: Push each set into loose soil with the pointed tip just showing at the surface, spacing them so the clusters have room to divide. Keep the bed weeded and steadily moist until the tops begin to yellow, then ease off water. Lift the whole clusters when the leaves fall over and cure the bulbs in a dry, airy place.

planting.note: A torpedo-shaped French shallot commonly grown from seed started indoors.

directions: Sow seed indoors in late winter and transplant clumps out in early spring, thinning to single plants for well-formed bulbs. Water evenly while the elongated, coppery-pink bulbs swell. Harvest when the tops yellow and topple, then cure in a dry spot for storage.

planting.note: A reliable round-bulbed hybrid shallot usually raised from seed.

directions: Start seed indoors and set seedlings out after hardening, spacing them for firm single bulbs. Keep the soil moist and weed-free through the growing season. Lift the coppery bulbs when the foliage dies down and cure them well for long keeping.

planting.note: A large, mild yellow shallot planted from sets in early spring.

directions: Set the bulbs into fertile ground with the tips at the surface as soon as the soil can be worked. Keep them evenly watered while the clusters multiply and the tops stay green. Harvest once the leaves flop and brown, then dry the golden-skinned bulbs thoroughly before storing.

planting.note: A single-stalk Japanese bunching onion sown thickly for long white shanks.

directions: Direct sow in shallow rows and hill soil against the stems to blanch a long white base. Keep the bed moist and free of weeds for tender, upright shanks. Pull the scallions once they reach usable thickness, staggering sowings for a continuous supply.

planting.note: A tall Japanese non-bulbing onion grown for long straight white stalks.

directions: Direct sow in a shallow drill and mound soil against the stems as they grow to lengthen the white portion. Water steadily and keep weeds down for clean, tender shanks. Pull the stalks any time from finger-thick onward.

planting.note: A scallion with striking purple stem bases that intensify in cool weather.

directions: Sow seed thinly in shallow rows and keep the soil evenly damp for steady growth. The purple coloring deepens as temperatures drop, so fall crops are the most vivid. Harvest the slender stalks young for the mildest flavor.

planting.note: A long-storing hybrid shallot commonly raised from seed.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant hardened seedlings once the ground is workable. Keep the bed evenly moist and weed-free while the reddish-brown bulbs form. Lift when the tops yellow and topple, then cure thoroughly for excellent winter storage.

planting.note: Sow in cool soil a few weeks before the last spring frost, and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Scatter seed about an eighth of an inch deep and keep the surface evenly moist until sprouts appear. Thin seedlings once they crowd, using the pulled plants as baby greens. Cut outer leaves as needed or harvest the whole plant before hot weather triggers bolting.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or start indoors for transplanting into cool soil.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep the bed moist while the upright plants form tall, sturdy heads. Water steadily to prevent bitterness and tip burn during warm spells. Harvest when the heads feel firm and dense, cutting at the base with a knife.

planting.note: Sow in cool weather; its compact size suits close spacing and containers.

directions: Plant seed a quarter inch deep and thin to give each little head room to fill out. Keep soil consistently moist for sweet, crunchy hearts. Cut the whole compact head once the center feels firm, well before summer heat.

planting.note: This cold-tolerant French heirloom shines as a fall and overwintering crop in mild zones.

directions: Sow shallowly in cool soil and keep evenly watered as the bronze-red heads develop. Cool weather deepens the red coloring and sweetens the flavor. Harvest young for salad leaves or let heads mature and cut at the base.

planting.note: A reddish heirloom romaine with good heat tolerance for late spring sowing.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep moist as the deep red-bronze heads grow upright. It holds in warm weather better than most, resisting early bolting. Cut the firm heads at the base once they reach full size.

planting.note: Very cold-hardy; excellent for fall planting and overwintering under cover.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and keep the soil cool and moist as the dense, dark green heads form. This variety endures frost well and can hold through mild winters with protection. Harvest whole heads or take outer leaves as a cut-and-come-again crop.

planting.note: An Austrian heirloom prized for green leaves speckled with maroon.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin as the spotted upright leaves develop. Keep it watered and harvest in cooler weather when the freckling is most vivid. Pick outer leaves young or cut mature heads at the soil line.

planting.note: A dependable heat-tolerant romaine for spring through early summer sowing.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep the bed evenly moist as large, dark green heads form. It resists bolting and tip burn better than many romaines in warm spells. Harvest the full heads by cutting at the base when firm and heavy.

planting.note: A widely grown butterhead noted for heat tolerance and slow bolting.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin to give each rosette space to form a loose, buttery head. Keep watering steady to preserve the tender, sweet leaves. Harvest the whole head when the center is soft and full, or pick outer leaves as needed.

planting.note: A classic small butterhead best grown in cool spring and fall weather.

directions: Plant shallowly and keep the soil cool and evenly moist as the compact heads form. Warm weather can turn it bitter and prompt bolting, so grow it in the cooler seasons. Harvest whole small heads once the loose center leaves cup inward.

planting.note: A tiny heirloom butterhead ideal for containers and tight spacing.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin to a few inches apart, since each plant only reaches tennis-ball size. Keep it moist and grow it quickly in cool weather. Harvest the entire miniature head as a single-serving lettuce.

planting.note: A hardy French heirloom that can be sown across multiple seasons.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep evenly watered as the ruby-tinged heads develop over green hearts. The red coloring intensifies in cool weather. Harvest whole heads when firm, or gather outer leaves as they mature.

planting.note: An Austrian summer butterhead bred to resist bolting in warmth.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin as the large, pale green heads form. Its heat tolerance lets you push sowings later into the season than most butterheads. Cut the soft, full heads at the base when they feel filled out.

planting.note: A large, reliable Bibb-type butterhead with good disease resistance.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep evenly moist as the big, glossy heads develop. Steady moisture keeps the folded leaves tender and sweet. Harvest whole heads once the centers cup firmly.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant; crispheads need a long cool season to firm up.

directions: Start seed indoors and set out young plants once the soil is workable, spacing them for full heads. Water consistently so the interior leaves wrap into a firm, crisp ball. Harvest when the head feels solid and heavy, before warm weather causes bolting.

planting.note: A classic crisphead that needs cool conditions and even moisture to head well.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into rich, cool soil with room to form heads. Keep watering even and steady, since drought stress causes bitterness and tip burn. Harvest once the head is firm and tightly packed.

planting.note: A popular British crisphead valued for large heads and slow bolting.

directions: Start indoors or sow shallowly and thin generously to allow the big heads to develop. Water consistently for crisp, well-wrapped centers with frilly outer leaves. Harvest when the heads feel firm and solid to the touch.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant crisphead bred to resist bitterness and tip burn.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into cool, fertile soil with wide spacing. Keep moisture even to encourage firm heads without a bitter edge. Cut the solid heads at the base once they reach full size.

planting.note: A crisphead selected to hold in warm weather without quickly bolting.

directions: Sow indoors or direct and thin to give heads room to fill. Its bolt resistance allows harvest later into summer than most crispheads. Pick the firm heads promptly once solid, since holding too long invites bolting.

planting.note: A fast, adaptable iceberg-type crisphead with good disease resistance.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant into cool soil, spacing plants for full heads. Keep moisture even to build firm, tightly folded centers. Harvest when heads are solid and before hot weather triggers bolting.

planting.note: A fast, cold-tolerant heirloom leaf lettuce ideal for early sowings.

directions: Broadcast or row-sow seed shallowly and keep the surface moist until it sprouts. Thin lightly and begin picking the light green outer leaves within a few weeks. Cut leaves repeatedly as a cut-and-come-again crop until the plant bolts.

planting.note: A frilly green leaf type long grown for greenhouse and early field crops.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in cool soil and keep evenly moist for quick germination. Harvest the ruffled, crinkled leaves young and often for the best texture. Cut outer leaves and let the center regrow, or pull the whole plant.

planting.note: An oakleaf-type looseleaf that resists bolting through warm weather.

directions: Scatter seed shallowly and thin as the deeply lobed green rosettes fill out. Its slow bolting lets you harvest over a long window. Pick outer leaves continually and the plant keeps producing from the center.

planting.note: A red-bronze oakleaf looseleaf with good bolt resistance.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin as the lobed, wine-red rosettes develop. Full sun and cool nights deepen the red coloring. Harvest outer leaves regularly for a long, ongoing cut.

planting.note: A tender, heat-tolerant heirloom named for its oak-shaped leaves.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep the bed moist as the lobed green rosettes fill in. It stays sweet and slow to bolt even as temperatures climb. Pick outer leaves as needed and let the plant keep producing.

planting.note: An Italian looseleaf grown for tightly frilled magenta-edged leaves.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin as the intensely ruffled rosettes form. Its frilly, red-tipped leaves add loft and color to salad mixes. Harvest outer leaves repeatedly, cutting near the base of each leaf.

planting.note: An award-winning bronze-red looseleaf that is especially slow to bolt.

directions: Scatter seed shallowly and thin as the crinkled bronze-red leaves expand. Its strong bolt resistance stretches the harvest into warmer weather. Pick outer leaves often to keep new growth coming from the center.

planting.note: A crinkly, glossy green looseleaf that holds well in the garden.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep moist as the savoyed, shiny green leaves develop. It stays crisp and sweet with a long harvest window. Take outer leaves as needed for a continuous cut.

planting.note: A tender heirloom looseleaf with red-tinged, crinkled leaves.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin as the frilly, bronze-edged leaves grow. The tender leaves are best picked young before they toughen. Harvest outer leaves continually until the plant begins to bolt.

planting.note: A vigorous red looseleaf with strong color even in warm weather.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep evenly moist as the ruffled, fire-red leaves expand. It resists bottom rot and holds color better than many red types in heat. Cut outer leaves regularly for an extended harvest.

planting.note: A hardy heirloom with distinctive triangular, tongue-shaped leaves.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin as the pointed leaves form a tight rosette. Its cold tolerance makes it dependable for early spring and fall crops. Pick outer leaves as needed or harvest the whole loose rosette.

planting.note: A pale chartreuse heirloom looseleaf noted for heat tolerance.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin as the large, ruffled light green leaves grow. It holds its sweet flavor and resists bolting in warm spells. Harvest outer leaves often for a long cutting season.

planting.note: A green looseleaf bred specifically for slow bolting in summer heat.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep moist as the crinkled green leaves fill in. Its resistance to bolting allows later sowings than most looseleaf types. Cut outer leaves regularly to prolong the harvest through warm weather.

planting.note: A deeply cut, frilly green looseleaf resembling endive in form.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin as the narrow, deeply notched leaves form a dense rosette. Its tangy flavor and lofty texture suit cut salad mixes. Harvest outer leaves often or cut the whole rosette an inch above the crown.

planting.note: An improved Black Seeded Simpson type with better bolt resistance.

directions: Broadcast or row-sow shallowly and keep moist until seedlings emerge. The light green, ruffled leaves stay sweet longer than the original Simpson. Pick outer leaves repeatedly for a cut-and-come-again harvest.

planting.note: The green counterpart to Lolla Rossa, with tightly curled leaves.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin as the frilly, bright green rosettes develop. Its ruffled leaves add volume and a mild flavor to salad blends. Harvest outer leaves as needed, cutting near the base of each stem.

common_name: Drunken Woman Frizzy Headed Leaf Lettuce

planting.note: An Italian heirloom with ruffled, bronze-tipped frizzy leaves.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep moist as the loose, frizzy rosettes with red-tinged edges expand. Cool weather sharpens the bronze coloring on the leaf margins. Pick outer leaves continually or cut the whole loose head.

planting.note: An Austrian heirloom romaine speckled with maroon, its name meaning trout-back.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep the soil evenly moist as the speckled green leaves form upright heads. The maroon splashing is most striking in cool weather. Harvest young leaves or cut the semi-heads at the base.

planting.note: A large green Bibb-type butterhead with good disease resistance.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep evenly moist as the smooth, glossy heads develop. It resists downy mildew and tolerates a range of conditions. Harvest whole heads once the soft centers fold inward.

planting.note: A Polish heirloom looseleaf with rosy-purple, upright leaves.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin as the tall, pink-veined leaves develop. Harvest the tender leaves young, as older ones can grow bitter as the plant ages. Pick outer leaves regularly to keep the rosette producing.

planting.note: Compact romaine-butterhead crosses well suited to close spacing.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to a hand's width so each small head fills out. Keep watering even for sweet, dense hearts. Harvest whole compact heads once firm, before warm weather sets in.

planting.note: Sow as soon as the soil can be worked in early spring, and again in late summer for fall harvest.

directions: Sow seed about half an inch deep in cool, rich soil and keep it evenly moist until sprouts appear. Thin young plants to a hand's width apart and side-dress with nitrogen once true leaves form. Cut the crinkled outer leaves as needed and harvest the whole plant before hot weather triggers bolting.

planting.note: A bolt-resistant hybrid best sown in earliest spring or for fall and overwintering crops.

directions: Plant seeds shallowly in fertile, well-drained ground and firm the soil for good contact. This upright, savoyed hybrid holds well in warm spells before bolting, so successive sowings extend the season. Snap off outer leaves regularly or harvest the entire rosette while leaves are still tender.

planting.note: Smooth-leaved hybrid that is quick and easy to wash; sow in spring and fall.

directions: Drill seed a half inch deep into cool soil and keep the bed moist during germination. The flat, spade-shaped leaves rinse clean easily, making this a favorite for baby-leaf salads. Begin cutting leaves when plants reach a few inches tall and pick the whole plant before summer heat.

planting.note: An especially cold-hardy strain grown for overwintering under cloches or mulch.

directions: Sow in late summer or early autumn so plants establish before hard frost. The broad, dark leaves tolerate freezing and can be picked through mild winter spells under a row cover. Harvest outer leaves lightly in winter, then enjoy a flush of new growth as days lengthen in spring.

planting.note: A slow-bolting savoy type good for both spring and fall plantings.

directions: Set seed a half inch deep in loose, fertile soil and keep the surface damp. This heavily crinkled variety resists bolting longer than many, giving a longer cutting window. Harvest outer leaves as they size up or cut the full plant at the base before it sends up a seed stalk.

planting.note: Smooth-leaf hybrid with strong disease resistance for spring and fall crops.

directions: Sow shallowly into cool, moist soil and thin seedlings once they crowd. The upright, flat leaves shed soil easily and resist downy mildew, so this variety suits dense baby-leaf beds. Cut leaves young for salad or let them grow larger for cooking, harvesting before heat sets in.
